planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall (cold stratification over winter) or transplant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile, loamy soil in full sun; Japanese walnut is allelopathic like other walnuts — its roots produce juglone, which inhibits growth of many plants beneath the canopy. Give adequate space (40–60 feet from other susceptible plants).

directions: Plant fresh nuts in fall or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile, loamy soil in full sun; Japanese walnut is an impressive large nut tree from the mountains of Japan, Hokkaido, Honshu, and Sakhalin — closely related to Persian walnut (Juglans regia) but growing in colder climates and producing rounder, harder-shelled nuts. The trees are immediately distinguished by their enormous compound leaves (the largest of any walnut — up to 4 feet long with 11–17 leaflets) and by the sticky glandular hairs covering young growth and fruit husks. The nuts are round to slightly oval, with a very hard, thick shell and rich, oily kernel. The variety 'cordiformis' (heartnut) produces a distinctively heart-shaped nut that cracks more easily than the round-nutted species. Used in Japanese cuisine and for nut oil. Like all walnuts, releases juglone from its roots that can inhibit many nearby plants. The closely related Manchurian walnut (J. mandshurica) is even hardier. An excellent, ornamental, large-canopy nut tree for cooler northern gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Chinese parasol tree is fast-growing and self-sows abundantly — seedlings are common in warm-climate gardens and the tree can become invasive in the southeastern US. Remove unwanted seedlings while small.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Chinese parasol tree (fenghuang tree or phoenix tree in Chinese, critical in Chinese mythology as the only tree on which the mythical fenghuang/phoenix would perch) is a fast-growing deciduous tree from China and Japan with extraordinary ornamental character — the smooth, bright gray-green bark is distinctive and attractive year-round, the enormous 3–5 lobed, maple-like leaves (up to 12 inches wide) create a bold, tropical appearance, and the unusual fruits (split open as boatlike follicles with edible seeds attached to the margins) are unlike any other temperate tree fruit. The seeds have a mild flavor and are eaten roasted in China. Widely planted across the southeastern US and has naturalized in many areas. The botanical family Malvaceae places it with hibiscus, cotton, and cacao. Beautiful spring flower spikes of greenish-yellow flowers. The pale gray bark is particularly attractive in winter, glowing almost white against a dark background. A dramatic, fast-growing ornamental but aggressive self-sower.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; royal paulownia is extremely fast-growing — potentially the fastest-growing hardwood tree in temperate cultivation. Self-sows abundantly and is listed as invasive in many eastern US states — remove seed capsules before they open to control spread.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; royal paulownia is the larger-flowered Chinese species of paulownia — a massive, fast-growing tree with enormous heart-shaped leaves (up to 18 inches) and spectacular large, fragrant, pale lilac-white, foxglove-shaped flowers in early spring (before the leaves emerge). The flowers are larger and paler in color than those of the more commonly planted princess tree (Paulownia tomentosa). One of the world's fastest-growing hardwood trees, capable of 10–20 feet of growth in the first year from seed and 8–15 feet per year subsequently in ideal conditions. The exceptionally light, strong, rot-resistant wood dries rapidly without warping and is highly valued in Japan (where it's called kiri) for making chests, musical instruments (koto), and wooden sandals. Produces billions of tiny seeds from its large, oval seed capsules — be vigilant about removing seed pods before they open to prevent invasive spread in moist eastern US forests where it has naturalized. More heat-tolerant than princess tree.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; purple three-awn grows naturally in hot, dry, open desert grassland, rocky slopes, and sandy plains throughout the Southwest and Southern Plains. Very drought tolerant — essentially no supplemental irrigation needed after establishment in appropriate climates.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; purple three-awn is the most widespread of the Aristida (three-awn) grasses of the western half of North America — a small to medium-sized warm-season bunchgrass named for the three awns (bristle-like extensions) on each seed that give the panicle its distinctive appearance. The purple-tinted seed heads in summer and fall are ornamental in mass plantings, creating a haze of fine purple-red color over large areas of desert grassland. An important native grass of the Chihuahuan Desert grassland, Sonoran Desert margins, and Southern Plains, often occurring in disturbed, degraded, or overgrazed soils where more palatable native grasses have been lost. Despite its common occurrence in disturbed sites, it is a valuable native for erosion control on dry, rocky, sandy slopes where more demanding species are difficult to establish. Three-awn grass seeds have been problematic for livestock and dogs — the sharp, twisted awns can penetrate skin and cause injury.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average, loamy or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; blue wild rye is one of the most shade-tolerant native bunchgrasses of western North America, naturally growing under open canopy oak woodland, mixed forest edges, and shaded slopes where few grasses survive.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average, loamy or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; blue wild rye is a cool-season native bunchgrass widely distributed across western North America from Alaska through California, the Rocky Mountains, and into the Great Plains — one of the very few native grasses that can establish and persist under the partial shade of oak woodland, mixed forest edges, and shaded north-facing slopes. The bluish-green (glaucous) foliage is distinctly attractive and gives the species its common name. The tall, nodding seed heads are produced in early summer. An outstanding native grass for shaded wildflower meadow projects, oak woodland understory plantings, and difficult shaded sites where most grasses and groundcovers struggle. Valued for erosion control on partially shaded slopes. Self-sows modestly in appropriate conditions. Matures early and becomes summer-dormant in drier areas, going dormant in summer and greening again with fall rains — a cool-season grass cycle typical of western grassland species.
